data.title: Apple: Forget The Narratives, There's A Bigger Reason Not To Own It Now
data.description:
Investors of all stripes are in danger of taking an outsized risk with the biggest stocks. Their assumption is that when a great company's stock price falls, say by 1/3 of its all-time high price, that's an automatic "buy the dip" situation. That's a lot of what we saw on Wednesday. But take it from a career stock price trend analyst, that alone is not a buy signal.

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has lost several billion dollars in market value, having just bounced up from its worst decline from an all-time high in several years (below). This was due to the uncertainties posed by tariffs, at least in part. But there's much more to the sanguine prospects with this iconic business's stock. The recent plunge in AAPL doesn't open up enough margin of safety to make us optimistic.

Here are 3 reasons to sell/not own Apple stock.

#1 Apple's Valuation Is Not Yet Attractive

Addressing the elephant in the room, yes, Apple is an incredible company that deserves a premium valuation to reward its quality, stability, capital allocation history, and everything else.

But the factor grades below sum it up a bit. In recent months, Apple's valuation has gone from bad to awful.

Even with this rich valuation, its growth also receives a D grade, which makes things even worse. Less tangible factors, such as tariffs and macroeconomic concerns, risk of recessions, and the like, have also worsened in recent months, and even with the drop, Apple's valuation remains stretched.

Its forward price-to-earnings is 25x considering fiscal year 2025, which is still above its historical average.

#2 Apple’s Growth Isn’t Compelling Either

Apple is already a very mature company. Although the smartphone market continues to grow, it is not expected to grow at a double-digit pace over the next few years, actually, according to Statista, the CAGR estimates are closer to 4% for the next few years.

The valuation may not seem so bad, since 25x forward earnings is in line with other tech companies and even better than some like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), but you have to look at it in relation to growth.

Due to these factors, the market estimates a revenue growth for Apple next year of 4.4%, and getting close to the mid-single digit in the coming years. This is quite coherent since services revenue should continue to grow at a faster pace and push revenue upwards, while product revenue, such as iPhone and Mac, should grow at a slower pace on average, more related to price increases and a slight increase in the user base.

Perhaps over the next couple of years, Apple will be able to show good growth numbers due to the iPhone cycle. But overall, this single-digit growth in the top line is not so attractive, especially if we think about the threats to this growth, such as tariffs, macroeconomic uncertainties, strong competition in regions such as China, and regulatory risks that could affect the main pillar of the thesis, services.

#3 Reason: Tariffs Enhance Uncertainty

The third reason is tariffs. To some, it may seem like noise, and really, no one knows exactly how this will play out over the next few years, but in any case, it creates a lot of uncertainty and volatility.

One of the feasible scenarios is that iPhones will become much more expensive, with price hikes of up to $350. And this certainly has the potential to affect the fundamentals of the top and bottom line.

With Apple raising prices to justify tariffs, this should cause demand to fall, especially with the scenario as volatile as it is, many will wait to upgrade their phones. Likewise, by absorbing part of these tariffs, the company also loses margin, i.e., a scenario may emerge in the coming quarters where revenue falls, and EPS falls even further.

In the medium term, the company should be able to resume growth, but there is a high cost to this adaptation. Migrating production, changing contracts, changing logistics, all of this has a high monetary cost in the short term.

Price trend analysis

The daily view here is a good news/bad news situation. It looks better than many stocks. But that's like saying you got a new phone, but it is an iPhone 2. The stock has a prayer of landing once again in the familiar $170 area, where it bottomed or topped 5 times since late 2021. While that is interesting, it is not yet significant.

The bigger issue could be in this very long-term price chart, which informs me that the 20-month moving average is in danger of rolling over. It has been so long that AAPL has been in this predicament, that I'd have to resize the chart to cut out the past 10 years just to find the last time that type of signal was brewing. So hopefully, that makes my point.

Crash…please?

This is a business I'd be thrilled to own if it does a "2013" all over again. I stubbornly waited (since my technical system was not as robust nor responsive as it is now) until the stock, then at single-digit earnings, was selling at a level that in hindsight, would have made my life a lot easier as a portfolio manager and advisor for the next 7 years or so.

I recently owned AAPL in collar form, but the recent meltdown, while limiting my losses quite a bit, only served to reinitiate me to being a hopeful AAPL holder. I think I'll get another chance, but I need more to make a new move here. I will assign a hold rating to AAPL because a sell would imply I'm very pessimistic about it. It is such a big allocation in the S&P 500 index, that it "is" the market to some degree. And I don't like this market, not here, not for now.

data.title: Broadcom: Maintaining Buy Rating On Surprising Upward Earnings Revisions
data.description:
Recently, I have updated my price targets on Apple (AAPL) and Nvidia (NVDA), and what I noted is that the price target adjustments were not so much driven by lower earnings in the years ahead as those revisions to the estimates were less than 2%.

The bigger adjustments to the price targets have come from applying lower EV/EBITDA multiples to account for the added risk in the current trade environment.

I covered Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) in March this year, and since then, the stock has fallen by 11.3%. In this report, I will briefly discuss the risks the companies face, and I will discuss how earnings estimates have changed. I will use those changed estimates and lower trading multiple to establish stock price targets and a rating for the stock.

Broadcom Has Significant Exposure To China

Broadcom is a fabless semiconductor company, and its products are manufactured in the U.S. mostly for its wireless communication equipment and fiber-optic products. Its advanced chips are built in Taiwan.

Technically, the tariffs that China imposed are only applicable to the shipments from the U.S. to China. However, the U.S. could impose even more strict export controls that could limit shipments from Taiwan to China and hurt Broadcom’s sales.

The added complexity and risk for Broadcom is that it does ship goods to China that are used to assemble products that are also destined for the U.S. Those products would be subject to the U.S. tariffs.

This brings the company in the position that it faces a direct impact from the tariffs from its chip products and an indirect impact from chips being implemented in end products. Higher tariffs are likely to erode demand for both, and the higher cost could eventually result in an increase in inflation, which would further erode demand. So, there is a real risk of disposable income and GDP growth declining, which could affect the sales.

Part of the company’s revenues come from the use of its chips in consumer goods and part of it comes from IT modernization projects. When the economy in the U.S. or China tanks, those sales come under pressure. The sales pressure is also not limited to U.S. and China. Due to the size of these economies, it could cause the global economic growth to flatten, which would hurt demand for Broadcom’s product (both semiconductor goods and services) in markets other than the U.S. and China.

According to Broadcom’s annual report (page 88), the company derived $11.53 billion of its sales from China. This is over 22% of the company’s full-year sales. There is no breakdown of sales to China for semiconductor sales and licenses, which are not subject to tariffs, but it is clear that Broadcom has substantial exposure to China. Part of those sales could be subject to tariffs and a potential tightening of export controls.

Previously, I believed that the company had some advantages in the sense that it derives its revenues from goods as well as services with long-term agreements in place. While I still do believe that those elements are a de-risk for Broadcom, the extent of the current trade war even when the U.S. does not impose tariffs on countries other than China brings significant risk to the global economy and puts Broadcom’s business globally at risk regardless of the shielding elements it has in place.

Recession fears have spiked to 79% and that recession fear can affect Broadcom sales with a subsequent hit to sales when a recession actually does happen. So, even if there is no substantial revision of earnings estimates, the risk of those estimates being revised downward in the near future does increase as chances of a recession loom large.

Earnings Estimates Have Increased For Broadcom

Whereas other companies that I covered showed a negative revision to EBITDA and free cash flow, this is not the case for Broadcom. EBITDA estimates have held rather well and are up 0.2% for 2025-2027 while free cash flow estimates are 0.3% higher. This does not mean that there may not be any negative revisions in the future, but it is a stronger sign for Broadcom that could be partially driven by its exposure to services sales.

Broadcom Stock Price Target Lowered

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Broadcom, I am reducing my stock price target from $252.82 to $214.24 implying 24% upside. That is a long-term price target. In the near-term, I do believe there could be downside to $146.84. It is not an extremely favorable risk profile, but based on the downside risk against the company median EV/EBITDA and the upside, I do believe maintaining the buy rating is justified.

Conclusion: Broadcom Remains Attractive

Broadcom definitely does face risk from direct implications of the trade war and indirect implications such as global economic growth tapering. So, it is not the case that Broadcom is free of risk. It has some partial shielding through sales of services which are not subject to tariffs, which obviously is a positive. That does not completely eliminate the risks.

As a result, I am reducing my price target from $252.82 to $214.24, but I do maintain my buy rating for the stock. I remain mindful about the risk to earnings and the uncertainty in the markets, as US tariffs are changing every other day.

data.title: YMAG: I Expect Long-Term Underperformance
data.description:
One of the most popular new investing trends in recent years has been the rise of high yield covered call ETFs. The idea of systemically selling call options on major market indexes such as the S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100 has been well-known for a while. However, ETF providers have launched increasingly diverse products which operate covered call strategies on more niche sets of assets and sectors.

YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG) takes this idea even further. It is a fund of fund which owns seven other YieldMax ETFs which sell covered calls on the so-called magnificent seven stocks. Thus, YMAG serves as a sort of one-stop shop for anyone wanting to sell covered calls on seven mega-cap tech stocks within one product wrapper. It's certainly a novel idea. But is it a good one?

I Believe YMAG Will Underperform Its Underlying Stocks Over Time

In a perfectly rational efficient market, options should be priced correctly, and thus there be no net return to either buyers or sellers of call options on average. In the real world, call options used to be generally overpriced versus underlying volatility, and there was actual alpha to be had be systematically selling call options. However, since this was fact was discovered and institutions began shorting large amounts of option volatility, this opportunity has largely dried up on the major indexes. While we have less long-term data to look at for individual stock volatility selling strategies, I believe a similar effect will play out.

And that's before considering that YMAG has an expense ratio of 1.12% and there can be considerable transaction costs and slippage associated with selling and rolling call options so frequently. All this will make it hard for YMAG to match, let alone exceed the performance of the underlying stocks it is trading unless there is large alpha in the option selling strategy. It's my contention that there is not.

Let me provide some evidence for that assertion. The chart below shows the total returns since inception for two YieldMax single stock option selling funds, which have shown extreme outlier returns.

The first of these is for vaccine maker Moderna (MRNA) whose shares have collapsed over the past year. And the second is for Strategy (MSTR), the Bitcoin-holding company whose shares have soared over the same time period. Both stocks are presented below along with their accompanying YieldMax option income strategy ETF:

As you can see, the YieldMax version underperformed the underlying equity in both cases. For Strategy, its shares are up 282% over this time period, whereas the YieldMax fund is up just 187%, and yes, that includes dividends.

Meanwhile, on the losing end of the spectrum, Moderna stock is down 75% since the YieldMax fund launched, while the YieldMax income version is down 76% including the dividends.

This shows that selling covered calls greatly caps your upside when a stock is moving sharply higher, as Strategy did last year. At the same time, selling covered calls does little to limit losses if a stock goes into a dramatic decline. Even with the dividends paid from the YieldMax MRNA Option Strategy ETF, it has slightly lagged the simpler approach of just owning Moderna stock outright. If you have equal losses in a downturn and smaller gains when the underlying stock goes up, that's not going to be a winning long-term formula.

Is Mag 7 The Best Basket To Sell Options Against?

There's nothing magical about the particular seven stocks that YMAG is selling options against. Over time, the market's winners and losers change. For YieldMax to maintain these seven particular companies at equal allocations, it will have to be continuously rebalancing the fund, cutting position sizes at the winning companies while averaging down on the ones that are losing.

Suppose you ran a fund that owned just the MSTR options income fund and the MRNA options income fund at a 50/50 split. Wouldn't be too bad, right, as the Strategy gains would offset the Moderna losses, right?

Not so fast. Because the fund would need to maintain a 50/50 exposure to the two holdings, it would have been constantly selling down the Strategy position to buy more Moderna over the past year. This doesn't work out well in the end if one of the companies in question goes into terminal decline.

Is it impossible to imagine any of the Magnificent 7 falling on permanent hard times? It's not as likely as Moderna running into trouble, I'll grant you that. But it's not inconceivable that, say, Tesla (TSLA) share could fall 80% from peak to trough at some point, particularly if competition from the likes of Rivian (RIVN) or BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) intensifies. In fact, TSLA stock has already slipped as much as 50% from its highs a quarter ago. Similarly, NVIDIA (NVDA) shares have slumped in recent weeks as analysts raise more doubts about long-term AI demand.

These seven particular companies in the YieldMax fund have been great performers in the past. But there's no guarantee that will continue in the future. And by rebalancing these seven stocks to maintain equal allocations, it creates a likely outcome where the fund will be throwing good money after bad at some point as one or more of these seven companies stops being so magnificent.

YieldMax Mag 7 Fund Historical Returns

How has YMAG performed so far compared to alternatives? Here it is versus the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100:

Granted, the above chart of historical returns since YMAG's launch doesn't look too bad. The fund has outperformed both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 since inception. So, what's the issue?

YMAG has barely outperformed its counterparts, even though it tends to be significantly more volatile when markets go both up and down. YMAG appeared to be significantly outperforming in the recent market rally up through December. But it has lost nearly all that alpha during the recent market correction. I believe YMAG would end up lagging the indexes to a meaningful degree if and when a more prolonged bear market ensues.

Total Returns Do Matter. Don't Be Blinded By Yields

One common argument in favor of YMAG in particular and ultra-high-yielding option strategy ETFs in general is that they give investors income without having to reduce their position sizes. The analogy of not eating one's seed corn is often employed.

And yet, does that logic hold up in practice? I'd argue that it doesn't. Take the aforementioned YieldMax Moderna income fund mentioned above. Here's its price chart:

But you still have all your shares, so there's no problem, right? MRNY stock paid out $5.65/share of dividends over the past 12 months and so as long as the dividends keep on flowing in, things should be okay.

Unfortunately for shareholders, that's not how it works out. Because the fund's assets are sharply declining, the amount of covered call income it can generate from selling options also declines sharply.

Just stop and consider the chart of MRNY's dividend payouts since January 2024:

In early 2024, MRNY was paying close to $1/share per month in dividends. Fast-forward a year, this has declined to less than 20 cents a share per month, and the figure continues to decrease as time marches onward. This is the textbook definition of a melting ice cube.

The case of Moderna is a particularly stark one, since that company seems like it was something of a one-hit wonder tied to the COVID-19 vaccine moment. Once that passed, so did the company's general business prospects.

However, it's informative to the extent of how many covered call funds work in general, albeit usually on a slower timeframe. Over time, the assets in a fund will be eroded. When a stock goes up, the fund's gains are capped. But when the stock drops, the fund's NAV takes a hit. The market over the past week is a perfect example of this, with huge rallies and massive declines that far exceeded the levels of volatility that option sellers were expecting. It's almost inevitable that the net asset value of such a structure is going to trend downward over time, and as NAV declines, so does the inherent income potential remaining at the fund.

People may argue that owning a product like YMAG allows them to have their cake and eat it too. But the income comes at the cost of erosion of one's invested capital.

Covered call sellers earn their premiums because there are options buyers that believe that they are going to make a profit buying those derivative instruments. And, over time, the options market usually more or less equals out between buyers and sellers. Sometimes one side or the other has a small house edge, but it tends to not be either permanent or especially dramatic.

Because option selling doesn't have a particularly large inherent edge (and is sometimes an outright losing strategy), this isn't income in the traditional sense. In practice, it feels a lot more like return of capital, where your initial investment is slowly being given back to you. There's nothing wrong with that. There are other financial assets, such as annuities, that are structured along similar grounds. But please be aware of the mechanics of how these sorts of covered call funds work. This is not a sustainable long-term dividend income stream of the sort you'd get from a company that has rising earnings and thus can pay rising dividends over time.

YMAG Verdict

It's my view that this fund of ETFs structure exposes investors to fairly high fees while maintaining an equal-weighted basket of stock exposures that may not be ideal over the long term.

YMAG is showing a particularly high quoted yield at the moment. But this is tied to the unusually high levels of implied volatility in the stock market as well. When the present volatility spike ends, so will the unusually large dividend payments from YMAG. Meanwhile, the recent market gyrations are likely to significantly erode the underlying net asset values of the funds in which YMAG invests.

I'm not necessarily against covered calls as an options' strategy. But I don't see this fund as being a particularly attractive way to get exposure to that theme.
